title: Variances
summary: 9609 AS — favourable and adverse variances, calculation, causes, and management responses.
learningObjectives: Calculate budget variances for costs and revenue.
Classify variances as favourable or adverse.
Suggest plausible causes from case evidence.
Recommend management responses to significant variances.

simpleExplanation.title: Actual vs plan
simpleExplanation.summary: A variance is the difference between budgeted and actual figures. Investigating variances helps managers control performance and take corrective action.
steps: Costs: favourable if actual < budget (spent less).
Revenue: favourable if actual > budget (earned more).
Investigate large adverse variances and act.

flashcards.back: Variance = Actual − Budget (for both costs and revenue).
Actual cost < Budget cost — spent less than planned (F).
Actual revenue > Budget revenue — sold more or higher price (F).
Actual revenue < Budget — underperformed vs plan (A).
Higher prices, waste, inefficiency, unfavourable exchange rate.
Higher productivity, lower wage rates, less overtime than planned.
Investigate root cause; revise budget; improve processes; supplier negotiation.

faq.q: Does F always mean good and A always mean bad?
faq.a: Usually, but context matters. Favourable revenue from deep discounting may harm long-run profit. Adverse R&D spend may be good if innovation succeeds. Link to the case.

faq.q: Should I use absolute values for variance?
faq.a: Show the signed difference and label F or A. Some texts use Budget − Actual; Cambridge 9609 uses Actual − Budget — stay consistent and label clearly.

sections.content: **Variances** (5.5.2) finish **AS Topic 5 Finance**. Every calculation question expects **F or A** and **one case-linked cause**. This connects to **5.4.3 cost control** and **5.5.1 budgets**.

Variance = Actual − Budget

**Costs:** F if actual < budget | A if actual > budget

**Revenue:** F if actual > budget | A if actual < budget

sections.question:
Budgeted labour cost $48 000; actual labour cost $52 500.
Budgeted sales revenue $200 000; actual sales revenue $215 000.

Calculate both variances and classify them.
sections.solution:
**Labour cost variance**
= $52 500 − $48 000 = **+$4 500** → **Adverse (A)** — spent more than budgeted.

**Sales revenue variance**
= $215 000 − $200 000 = **+$15 000** → **Favourable (F)** — revenue above budget.

Net effect on profit: need to consider whether higher sales caused extra labour or if inefficiency drove cost overrun — use case details.

sections.question:
Budgeted material cost $30 000; actual $27 600. Case states supplier offered an unexpected bulk discount.

Calculate the variance and explain a likely cause.
sections.solution:
Variance = $27 600 − $30 000 = **−$2 400** → **Favourable (F)** — material costs below budget.

**Cause (from case):** Bulk purchase **discount from supplier** reduced unit material cost below the budget assumption.

items: **Revenue A:** lower volume, price cuts, competition, poor marketing.
**Revenue F:** strong demand, successful promotion, new market.
**Cost A:** inflation, waste, inefficiency, quality problems, overtime.
**Cost F:** productivity gains, cheaper suppliers, economies of scale.

sections.content: Managers **investigate material adverse variances** — revise budgets if assumptions changed permanently, or fix operations if inefficiency is the cause. **Persistent favourable variances** may mean the budget was too easy (motivation issue).
Always write: "Variance = $X **Adverse** because…" and quote **evidence from the case** (e.g. "as stated, machine breakdown increased overtime"). Generic causes earn fewer marks.
